Ehh, this is a dubious narrative. Twitter is down 17% from right before when Elon bought a stake on April 4, causing the stock price to spike. But it's been a horrible period for tech stocks and the NASDAQ is down 20% in the same period. https://www.nytimes.com/... https://twitter.com/...

State of play: 1/ general legal consensus is that Twitter has upper hand on Elon, based on what's currently known. 2/ Twitter will look even stronger after it files complaint, because plaintiffs almost always do. 3/ real intrigue begins when Elon (via his lawyers) reply.

In a new letter, Twitter's lawyers respond to Musk's lawyers saying, essentially, 1.) no the deal is not terminated, 2.) your client has not suffered a material adverse effect, and 3.) we will do what is necessary (i.e. sue) in order to complete the deal. https://www.sec.gov/...
Twitter's lawyers get their turn: “...Mr. Musk and the other Musk Parties have knowingly, intentionally, willfully, and materially breached the Agreement...” https://twitter.com/...

I feel like there's real overlap in what “bots” do for Elon and what “Obama's birth certificate” did for Trump ... a conspiracy theory that rallies partisans, justifies bad behavior, and can never be disproven to the huckster's satisfaction
@CaseyNewton also, people use “bots” now to mean “troll accounts of a political persuasion that opposes mine”. Constantly.

Many smarter ppl than me have said this but it's worth repeating that ‘bot’ is a term w functionally no meaning for platforms. You get all kinds of signals and then draw an educated line where you've decided behavior is authentic enough to allow. It's an artificial binary.

@CaseyNewton it's not just Elon. it's a convenient boogeyman. Are there bots? yes. Are they evenly distributed? Seems like no - he prob sees all of the crypto bot trash, that mostly slides into the replies of prominent people w/crypto audiences. Also, this dynamic: https://twitter.com/...
“.. the possible outcomes of litigation are extremely terrible..” @matt_levine $TWTR https://www.bloomberg.com/... https://twitter.com/...

So the possible outcomes of litigation are extremely binary: Elon Musk pays $44 billion and buys Twitter, or he pays zero-to-$1-billion and does not. And there is nothing in between https://www.bloomberg.com/... https://twitter.com/...

“If they can work out a deal like this — Musk pays Twitter $5 billion or $10 billion or whatever to walk away — then that seems better to me than having a disgruntled Musk buy Twitter at a lower price,” Levine writes. Do you agree? Should Elon... https://www.bloomberg.com/...

But because the outcomes are so drastically different, and so binary, and so bad, and because no one is sure which one it will be, the two sides have a lot of incentive to try to settle for something in between. There are two possible sorts of settlement: https://www.bloomberg.com/... https://twitter.com/...

“I will ignore the first possibility, not only because I think it is unlikely but also because it is functionally the same as the second,” Levine writes. In either case, Musk is fine, and Twitter is not https://www.bloomberg.com/...
The outcomes of a legal fight between Musk and Twitter are so binary, and so bad, that they should probably settle. https://www.bloomberg.com/...
If the Elon Musk-Twitter dispute ends up in court, there are three things that the court can do, writes @matt_levine https://www.bloomberg.com/... https://twitter.com/...

On the surface it sounds compelling. Apple already sells the most expensive purchase most people make after their house and car. As the iPhone reaches saturation, automobiles can be the next huge revenue growth driver. It needs software & manufacturing savvy which they have.

Question is how does Apple differentiate when there are already luxury car brands (Benz, BMW, etc), EV brands (Tesla, Rivian) and self driving brands (Waymo, Cruise). There isn't a wedge to fit in and they don't have the training data of Tesla or Waymo to compete on self driving

The source of the #Uberfiles has come forward to explain why he leaked 124,000 documents to the Guardian. He is Mark MacGann, formerly Uber's chief lobbyist in Europe, the Middle East and Africa. Here's his exclusive interview with us. https://www.theguardian.com/ ...

It's the second day of #UberFiles revelations as ICIJ, @guardian, and more than 40 global media partners blow the lid on the covert business strategies of one of the biggest and most influential tech startups of our time. 🧵 A recap of today's bombshells: https://www.icij.org/... https://twitter.com/...
“It felt like children playing around with powerful surveillance technology...” https://twitter.com/...
Holy moly! Honestly I don't know how Uber ever wants to be taken seriously again as a proper company that follows the law after these revelations. #UberFiles https://www.theguardian.com/ ...

“I regret being part of a group of people which massaged the facts to earn the trust of drivers, of consumers and of political elites ... It is my duty to [now] speak up and help governments and parliamentarians right some fundamental wrongs.” https://www.theguardian.com/ ...

“When (claims that drivers were going to benefit) turned out not to be the case - we had actually sold people a lie - how can you have a clear conscience if you don't stand up and own your contribution to how people are being treated today?” https://www.theguardian.com/ ...

“A leaked trove of confidential files has revealed the inside story of how the tech giant Uber flouted laws...” The Guardian leads a group of 180+ journalists at 40 media properies including Le Monde, Washington Post, and the BBC. All will do Uber stories. https://www.theguardian.com/ ...

So... there is plenty of actually damning stuff in The Uber Files, so this isn't an attempt to minimize it. But so many people are focused on the “we're pirates” quote which I think means a lot less than people think... https://www.theguardian.com/ ... https://twitter.com/...

“The company approach in these places was essentially to break the law, show how amazing Uber's service was, and then change the law. My job was to go above the heads of city officials, build relations with the top level of government, and negotiate.” https://www.theguardian.com/ ...
And from the International Consortium of Investigative Journalists, which helped organize and lead the collaboration of more than 180 journalists worldwide. https://www.icij.org/...

I'm surprised Uber only opened in Malta in June 2022. “Uber was adept at finding unofficial routes to power, applying influence through friends or intermediaries... [it] enlisted the backing of powerful figures ... by [..] turning them into “strategic investors”. https://twitter.com/...
Worth thinking about if you use Uber https://twitter.com/...

Source of the Uber leaks goes public: “I am partly responsible. I was the one talking to governments, I was the one pushing this with the media, I was the one telling people that they should change the rules because drivers were going to benefit...” https://www.theguardian.com/ ...

Proud to have contributed to #UberFiles as they revealed not only the systemic proximity between corporate & political power but also the embarrassing inadequacy of our legal and political culture to address the former. More on @BBCPanorama tomorrow https://www.bbc.com/...

in the #UberFiles trove of 124k documents, one text message stands out. as Uber drivers were at risk of attacks by taxi drivers, former CEO Travis Kalanick assured colleagues that Uber may benefit from such violence. story from @byaaroncdavis https://www.washingtonpost.com/ ... https://twitter.com/...

“Uber executives also sought to deflect inquiries about the company's aggressive tax avoidance strategies by volunteering to help host countries collect income taxes owed by drivers, documents show.” @CICTAR_Tax https://twitter.com/...

The European Commission is set for a fresh uproar over lobbying and revolving doors as internal Uber documents reveal the role of former Dutch Commissioner Neelie Kroes in lobbying for Uber before she joined the company. Uber employees were instructed to keep it a secret: https://twitter.com/...

Leaked confidential files reveal how @Uber flouted laws, duped police, exploited violence against drivers and secretly lobbied governments during its aggressive global expansion, reports the @guardian. https://www.theguardian.com/ ...
Uber has acted since Day One as if it did not have to obey laws. When law breaking is your first principle, there is no governor on behavior. https://twitter.com/...

Today Klarna announces an $800m financing round during the worst stock downturn and challenging macro in decades. We are not immune to public peers being down 75-90% and hence our valuation is down on par. Here are some facts that the media might omit in reporting on this 🧵 👇
It's official: Klarna confirms it has raised $800m in new financing at a $6.7bn valuation - down a whopping 85% from last year https://www.klarna.com/...

Klarna press release: our valuation is up $4.6 B, or 219%, since 2018, better than our competitors. Unstated in Klarna press release: Klarna raised roughly $3.5 B in additional cash since 2018 (per pitchbook) https://www.klarna.com/... https://twitter.com/...

NO Founder should think nor accept that this is a proxy for his/her company...It's just the result of arrogance. Did not need to be like this & this is the real lesson for start-up founders. @Klarna's valuation slashed massively.... https://www.ft.com/...

The last project I worked on at FAIR London is out 🚀🎉 We build an AI system which helps editors make Wikipedia citations better. (2/2) As a knowledge source for our model, we created Sphere, a retrieval engine comprised of 134 million public webpages — an order of magnitude larger and significantly more intricate than ever used for this sort of research. Read more: https://ai.facebook.com/... https://twitter.com/...

A new AI model produced by the @MetaAI team will improve the quality of Wikipedia entries by checking citations against over 134 million web ages stored in a new library called Sphere. https://techmonitor.ai/...
